revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[hu:Dzsakkóin]][[ja:寂光院]][[zh:寂光院]]
{{ill|Japanese temple|ja|日本の寺院|Name = {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | image = [[File:JyakkouinHondo.jpg|260px]] | image description = Main Hall (rebuilt June 2005) | Location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} {{ill|Ōhara|ja|大原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhara|1=oohara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11433107}} Kusabusa-chō 676 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|7|26.76|N|135|49|15.75|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map 2 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|lat_deg=35|lat_min=7|lat_sec=26.76|lon_deg=135|lon_min=49|lon_sec=15.75|label=寂光院|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=11|position=center|width=200|float=center}} | Mountain name (Sangō) = {{ill|Seikōzan|ja|清香山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikōzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Honorific temple name (Ingō) = {{ill|Jakkō-in|ja|寂光院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jakkō-in|qq=}} | Principal image (Honzon) = {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}} | Sect = {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} | Temple Ranking = | Year of foundation = Tradition: in the 2nd year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (594) | Founder = | Originator = Tradition: by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} | Pilgrimage number = {{ill|Shinbutsu Pilgrimage Route|ja|神仏霊場巡拝の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Pilgrimage Route|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10444949}} No. 105 (Kyoto No. 25) | Cultural Properties = Jizō Bosatsu standing statue (designated as {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) | Official website = | Official website name = }} '''Jakkō-in''' (寂光院) is a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} temple (nunnery) located in the Ōhara area of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}’s {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}. Its mountain name is {{ill|Seikōzan|ja|清香山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikōzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and its temple name is {{ill|Yuquan Temple|en|Yuquan Temple|qid=Q11572692|lt=Gyokusen-ji|1=Yuquan Temple}}. Its principal image is {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. Tradition holds that its founding is credited to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, and it is famed as the refuge of {{ill|Kenrei-mon'in Tokuko|ja|建礼門院徳子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenrei-mon'in Tokuko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平徳子|1=Taira no Tokuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3315855}} (daughter of [[Taira no Kiyomori]]{{citation needed |Taira no Kiyomori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Kiyomori|qq=}}) after the fall of the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heike|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢平氏|1=Ise Heishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15734686}}, as recounted in the {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=Heike Monogatari|1=The Tale of the Heike}}.
== History == The precise origins of Jakkō-in remain unclear. According to temple tradition, it was founded in the 2nd year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (594) by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} in order to honor the soul of his father, {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|qid=Q349281|lt=Yōmyō Tennō|1=Emperor Yōmei}}, with its original name being {{ill|Yuquan Temple|en|Yuquan Temple|qid=Q11572692|lt=Gyokusen-ji|1=Yuquan Temple}} and with the first abbot being Tamateruhi-hime (also known as {{ill|Keizen-ni|ja|恵善尼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keizen-ni|1=Ezen-ni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11492348}}), who served as the prince’s wet nurse. However, Edo-period local histories also offer an alternative founding legend attributing the temple’s establishment either to the monk {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Tomeisho|ja|都名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomeisho|1=Miyako meisho zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11643495}}) or to Ryōnin, the progenitor of the {{ill|Yūzū Nembutsu|ja|融通念仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūzū Nembutsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 融通念仏宗|1=Yuzu Nembutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q578719}} sect who is said to have taken refuge in Ōhara and completed the Ōhara Declaration ({{ill|Shōmyō|en|Shōmyō|qid=Q1760256|lt=Shōmei|1=Shōmyō}}) at the end of the 11th century. Today, Jakkō-in is chiefly known as the site associated with the exile of {{ill|Kenrei-mon'in|ja|建礼門院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenrei-mon'in|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平徳子|1=Taira no Tokuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3315855}} as recounted in the {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=Heike Monogatari|1=The Tale of the Heike}}.
In the first year of the {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|qid=Q1194415|lt=Bunji|1=Bunji (era)}} era (1185), after the downfall of the Heike at the Battle of {{ill|Dan-no-ura|ja|壇ノ浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dan-no-ura|1=Dan-no-ura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10931453}}, the surviving consort of {{ill|Emperor Takakura|en|Emperor Takakura|qid=Q357261|lt=Kōkuratennō|1=Emperor Takakura}}, who was the mother of {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|qid=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}}, took refuge at the temple under the guidance of the nun {{ill|Awanai no Shiro|ja|阿波内侍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awanai no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (daughter of {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinori|en|Fujiwara no Michinori|qid=Q711600|lt=Shinsei|1=Fujiwara no Michinori}}), later taking the vows as Shinnyo-kishin and becoming the temple’s second abbot. Subsequently, she is reputed to have served as the model for the “Ōhara Onna.” Also, the wife of {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|en|Taira no Shigehira|qid=Q710537|lt=Taira no Shigehira|1=Taira no Shigehira}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Sukeko|ja|藤原輔子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sukeko|1=Fujiwara no Hoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11623576}}, became a nun and served Tokuko at the temple.
The Ōhara region, home also to {{ill|Sanzen-in|en|Sanzen-in|qid=Q1072538|lt=Sanzenn-in|1=Sanzen-in}}, was historically a training ground for Nembutsu devotees and a refuge for aristocrats. The story of Tokuko’s steadfast prayer for the repose of the Heike and the two emperors moved even {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|ja|後白河法皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 後白河天皇|1=Go-Shirakawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q317984}} to visit Jakkō-in in the second year of the Bunji era (1186)—a narrative recounted in the “Ōhara Miyuki” section of the Heike Monogatari as a symbol of the Buddhist teaching of impermanence.
The main hall was reconstructed during the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} era (1596–1615) by order of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|qid=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}}, with Katagiri Katsumoto as commissioner. However, on May 9, 2000 (Heisei 12), it was destroyed by arson＜ref＞{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/shobo/page/0000214903.html|title=Sakyō Ward: Jakkō-in Fire|publisher=Kyoto City Fire Department|date=2017-02-22|accessdate=2019-08-09}}＜/ref＞ (the perpetrator remains at large; the statute of limitations was declared in May 2007 (Heisei 19)). At that time, the main image—the wooden standing statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}} (an Important Cultural Property)—and paper‐mâché statues of Tokuko and Awanai no Shiro (crafted using letters and sutras associated with Kenrei-mon'in) were also damaged. The current main hall was rebuilt on June 2, 2005 (Heisei 17), and newly crafted images of the main image and of Tokuko and Awanai no Shiro were installed.
Outside the temple, to the east stands the Ōhara Seiryō (a mausoleum dedicated to Kenrei-mon'in Tokuko). Originally located within the temple grounds, it was later separated and placed under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} after the Meiji period. On the west side of the grounds lie the graves of Awanai no Shiro and others.
== Grounds == * Main Hall – Rebuilt on June 2, 2005 (Heisei 17). * Riverbank Pool * Pool facing all four sides * Study Hall * Bell Tower – A bell symbolizing impermanence is suspended. * Ruins of the Kenrei-mon'in hermitage – The remains of the hermitage in which Kenrei-mon'in Tokuko once resided. * Mountain Gate * Treasure Hall – Known as “Hōchi Matsuden,” it was opened in October 2006 (Heisei 18). * Tea Room “Koyun” * Garden * Cloister
== Main image == === Old Main image === The old wooden standing statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}} (an Important Cultural Property) stands 256.4 cm tall. An inscription enclosed within the statue records the year as the first year of {{ill|Kangi|en|Kangi|qid=Q1194200|lt=Kanki|1=Kangi}} (1229). On the shelves surrounding the statue and within its inner cavity, numerous smaller Jizō statues are enshrined – said to number 30,000 on the shelves and 30,000 within – earning the old main image the epithet “60,000-stature Jizō Bosatsu.” During the 2000 fire (Heisei 12), while the main body was damaged, the enclosed items survived; they continue to be collectively designated as the “Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Standing Statue (Damaged by Fire)” (an Important Cultural Property)＜ref＞{{Cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/193586|title=Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Standing Statue (Damaged by Fire)|publisher=Cultural Heritage Database|accessdate=2019-08-09}}＜/ref＞. It is now housed in a repository on higher ground and is open to the public on designated days only. === New Main image === The new main image was produced over three and a half years by the National Treasure Repair Institute of the Art Institute and was completed in 2005 (Heisei 17). Made in a mosaic style from [[hinoki]] wood, it faithfully replicates the appearance of the old main image at the time of its reconstruction. The paper‐mâché statues of Kenrei-mon'in and Awanai no Shiro, formerly in the old main hall, were remade in wood during the reconstruction.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Standing Statue (Damaged by Fire) and its Enclosed Items: ** Silk-based, hand-painted inscription (scroll) from the first year of {{ill|Kangi|en|Kangi|qid=Q1194200|lt=Kanki|1=Kangi}} (old calendar November 5) – 1 volume ** Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Standing Statue – 1 unit ** Key Lotus Sutra items, etc., in 5 volumes (including 1 inner volume inscribed on old calendar September 23 of the first year of Kanki) ** 1 sheet of wrapping paper bearing binding inscriptions ** 1 package of tea-scented silk sachets (in a wooden box) ** A set of jewels (in a wooden box) ** 1 sword (with hilt and scabbard) ** 1 sheet of cloth with a split peony pattern in green ** 11 coins from the Tang and Song dynasties ** 1 piece of wood fruit ** 1 piece of animal hide ** 1 sheet of raw cotton * (Items stored in wooden boxes:) ** 3,416 units of the wooden Jizō Bosatsu Standing Statue ** 3 units of boxed (kura–ko) wooden Jizō Bosatsu statues ** 1 horizontal flute ** A broken piece of bat fan (about one handful) ** 1 fragmentary inscription ** Appendix: Collective fragments of the wooden Jizō Bosatsu statue
The main image was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1986 (Showa 61), with the enclosed items added in 1988 (Showa 63) and an additional 3,210 small Jizō statues enshrined around it in 1989 (Heisei 1). Its designation name was updated in 2001 (Heisei 13) after the fire＜ref＞The relevant official government notices are: * Notice No. 88, Showa 61 (June 6) * Notice No. 78, Showa 63 (June 6) * Notice No. 93, Heisei 1 (June 12) * Notice No. 112, Heisei 13 (June 22)＜/ref＞.
== Nearby Sacred Sites == ; {{ill|Shinbutsu Pilgrimage Route|ja|神仏霊場巡拝の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Pilgrimage Route|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10444949}} : 104 {{ill|Kifune Shrine|en|Kifune Shrine|qid=Q276779|lt=Kifune Shrine|1=Kifune Shrine}} – '''105 Jakkō-in''' – 106 {{ill|Sanzen-in|en|Sanzen-in|qid=Q1072538|lt=Sanzenn-in|1=Sanzen-in}}
== Access == A 15-minute walk from the {{ill|Kagawa Bus|ja|香川バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa Bus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} “Ōhara” bus stop. (Note: Although there is a “Jakkō-in Michi” bus stop 12 minutes away, only one bus per year (on the Vernal Equinox) stops there.)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Tomomitsu Komatsu|year=2005|title=Art Treasures of Jakkō-in|volume=Map Edition|edition=8th|publisher=Henromichi Preservation Cooperative}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Taira no Tokuko|en|Taira no Tokuko|qid=Q3315855|lt=Kenrei-mon'in Tokuko|1=Taira no Tokuko}} (Kenrei-mon'in) * {{ill|National Heike Society|ja|全国平家会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Heike Society|1=National Heike Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11389374}} * {{ill|List of Buddhist temples in Japan|en|List of Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q10311993|lt=List of Japanese Temples|1=List of Buddhist temples in Japan}} *
== External Links == * {{official website|www.shikoku88-6869.com/jiin.html|Shi11ōzan Jinne-in Kannon-ji}} (Official temple website) * {{url|https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/68jinnein/|68th Sacred Site, Shi11ōzan Jinne-in}} (Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage Official Site)
{{translated page|ja|寂光院|version=100947276|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:寂光院]]
